Herein, a series of fullerene-free ternary solar cells have been fabricated based on a wide bandgap acceptor, IDTT-M, together with a wide bandgap donor polymer PM6 and a narrow bandgap NFA Y6.
Results and Discussions Ternary solar cells were fabricated with a conventional configuration (Figure 1a) and chemical structures of PM6, Y6 and IDTT-M were shown in Figure 1b.

(a) Representative J-V curves of the binary and ternary solar cells, (b) the EQE curves of solar cells, (c) the differences in EQE spectra and the legends indicating weight ratios of PM6:Y6:IDTT-M, (d) the Jph-Veff curves of typical solar cells, (e) the dependence of JSC on light intensity of typical
that IDTT-M and Y6 are well mixed and closely packed.

morphological studies indicate that at optimized blend ratio, the high crystallinity of IDTT-M can be mitigated and forms intermixed phases within the Y6 domain, which also helps to orient PM6 to the favorable face-on packing that is advantageous for vertical charge transport and improved hole mobility.
A highly crystalline and wide bandgap electron acceptor, IDTT-M is used to fabricate high efficiency ternary solar cells with PM6 and another narrow bandgap electron acceptor Y6.

things.name: EEA

things.name: hydroxyapatite (HA)
things.description: A calcium phosphate-based bioceramic with the chemical formula Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2, used in bone tissue engineering due to its osteoconductive properties and compositional similarity to bone mineral.

context_snippets.text: Calcium phosphate-based bioceramics such as hydroxyapatite (HA); Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2; β-tricalcium phosphate (β-TCP); Ca3(PO4)2; and biphasic calcium phosphate (BCP) — a mixture of HA and β-TCP — are composed of the same ions as bone, and have received most attention for bone repair applications.

things.name: β-tricalcium phosphate (β-TCP)

things.name: posterior segment
things.description: The rear portion of the telson structure, constituting most of the telson volume and bearing primary mechanical loads, exhibiting higher stiffness than anterior segments.

context_snippets.text: Comparison of the compressive stiffness, measured by the slope of the load-displacement curve, shows that the smasher telson has a substantially higher stiffness than that of the spearer for both anterior and posterior segments under both dried and hydrated conditions.

things.name: quasi-static compression test
things.description: A mechanical testing method conducted at a low strain rate (~10^-3 s^-1) to evaluate material response under near-equilibrium loading conditions.

context_snippets.text: Quasi-static compression tests (strain rate ~10-3 s-1) on the side of a freshly prepared and hydrated telson were conducted using a dactyl club as the compression loading head to mimic the natural loading scenarios (Figures 5D and E).

things.name: load-displacement curve
things.description: A graphical representation of the force applied to a material versus its deformation, used to determine mechanical properties such as stiffness.

context_snippets.text: Comparison of the compressive stiffness, measured by the slope of the load-displacement curve, shows that the smasher telson has a substantially higher stiffness than that of the spearer for both anterior and posterior segments under both dried and hydrated conditions.

things.name: water
things.description: A chemical compound that acts as a plasticizer in biological tissues by forming hydrogen bonds between polymer chains, reducing stiffness and increasing extensibility.

context_snippets.text: Hydrated biological tissues typically have higher extensibility and lower stiffness as compared to the dry state, due to water’s ability to act as a plasticizer by forming hydrogen bonds between the polymer chains of organic fibers.

things.name: medial ridge
things.description: A central ridge structure in the smashing-type telson that provides out-of-plane stiffness.

context_snippets.text: A primary medial ridge in the smashing telson provides out of plane stiffness, whereas the presence of ridges called carinae, located at the dorsal surface, suggest a role in imparting compliance for energy absorption.

things.name: 3D printed telson cross-sectional mimics
things.description: Biomimetic structures fabricated via 3D printing that replicate the cross-sectional geometry of mantis shrimp telson for mechanical testing.

things.name: stereolithography printer (Form 2, FormLabs, USA)
things.description: A 3D printing system that uses ultraviolet light to cure photopolymer resins layer by layer into three-dimensional objects.

context_snippets.text: Biomimetic structures mimicking the 2D cross-sectional curvatures of the smasher and spearer telson were 3D printed using a desktop stereolithography printer (Form 2, FormLabs, USA) with a clear methacrylate-based photopolymer resin.

things.name: CAD files
things.description: Computer-aided design files containing precise geometric models used for 3D printing and finite element analysis.

context_snippets.text: CAD files of the telson cross-sectional geometries were modeled using SolidWorks (Dassault Systèmes, France).

things.name: Finite element analysis
things.description: A computational method for simulating the mechanical behavior of materials and structures under load using discretized mesh models.

context_snippets.text: Design of CAD geometries and Finite element analysis: CAD geometries used in the 3D Finite element analysis were created from the micro-CT scans of the smasher telson.

things.name: ABAQUS/Standard
things.description: A finite element analysis software suite used for solving linear and nonlinear structural problems under static and dynamic conditions.

context_snippets.text: Simulations were performed with ABAQUS/Standard with meshes consisting of about 200,000 C3D4 elements.

things.name: Thermogravimetric analysis
things.description: An experimental technique that measures the mass change of a material as a function of temperature, used to study thermal stability and decomposition behavior.

context_snippets.text: Thermogravimetric analysis of powdered telson samples.

things.name: cuticle thickness
things.description: The thickness of the outer layer (cuticle) of the telson, influencing mechanical stiffness and compliance.

context_snippets.text: Comparison of telsons from two evolutionarily divergent species reveal differences in macro-morphology, cuticle thickness, and mineralization, imparting compressive stiffness as well as compliance for energy absorption.

things.name: mineralization
things.description: The process of depositing minerals within the telson structure, contributing to compressive stiffness and energy absorption.

context_snippets.text: Comparison of telsons from two evolutionarily divergent species reveal differences in macro-morphology, cuticle thickness, and mineralization, imparting compressive stiffness as well as compliance for energy absorption.

things.name: 3D printed telson geometries
things.description: Biomimetic 3D printed structures inspired by the tail segments of mantis shrimp, used to study mechanical performance under compression.

things.name: compression testing
things.description: An experimental technique used to evaluate the mechanical response of materials under compressive load, measuring stiffness and deformation behavior.
